coredata.dc:title: Chiral fluorescent carbon dots for tyrosine enantiomers: Discrimination, mechanism and cell imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yangxia
Kou, Manchang
Zhang, Haixia
Qiu, Hongdeng
Shi, Yan-Ping
coredata.dc:description: The development of chiral analytical methods for tyrosine enantiomers, characterized by superior recognition performance and selectivity, holds significant potential for application in the biochemical and biomedical domains associated with tyrosine. Herein, a straightforward and effective strategy for tyrosine enantiomers identification and purity analysis by chiral carbon dots (CDs) fluorescence sensor were reported. The chiral CDs were designed by using L-cysteine and 2,4-diaminophenol hydrochloride through a gentle one-step synthesis strategy at room temperature. The resulting chiral CDs exhibited completely different fluorescence response to L-tyrosine and D-tyrosine, that is, L-tyrosine could enhance the fluorescence of the chiral CDs, but D-tyrosine made no difference. The fluorescent differential recognition factor for enantiomers was as high as 23.2, which was the highest be achievement to our best knowledge so far. The varying discriminatory capabilities of chiral CDs towards tyrosine enantiomers were also applicable to HeLa cells. To confirm the recognition mechanism of chiral CDs to tyrosine enantiomers, the chiral CDs-modified SiO2 stationary phase were synthesized and applied to separate the tyrosine enantiomers in reverse-phase HPLC mode. Furthermore, density functional theory was combined with the fluorescence spectroscopy and chromatographic results to verify that the recognition ability was related to the difference of weak interaction energy and Gibbs free energy between the precursor molecule of the chiral CDs and the tyrosine enantiomers. This cost-effective and highly selectivity assay not only provided a valuable tool for tyrosine enantiomers identification and purity analysis, but also delivered an important reference for the recognition and purity identification of other chiral drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral recognition
Chiral fluorescent carbon dots
Enantiomeric differentiation
HPLC
Density functional theory calculations

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of chiral CDs-modified silica stationary phase
Preparation and characterizations of the chiral CDs
Preparation and characterization of chiral CDs-modified silica stationary phase
Sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescence method to identifytyrosine enantiomers
Recognition of L-tyrosine in real samples
Application of chiral CDs in the racemic solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image; (B) Particle size distribution histogram; (C) SAED pattern; (D) XRD pattern of the chiral CDs; (E) XPS survey spectrum; (F-I) High-resolution XPS spectrum of S 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s peak.
HeLa cells treated with (A-C) chiral CDs, (D-F) chirals CDs + D-tyrosine, and (G-I) chiral CDs + L-.
tyrosine for 2 h at 37 °C, respectively (the concentration of chiral CDs was 400 μg/mL).
Schematic diagram of preparation of chiral CDs and discrimination of tyrosine enantiomers together with cell imaging.
A comparison of the chiral CDs sensor with others for the recognition of tyrosine enantiomers.
Detection of L-tyrosine in milk samples.

head.ce:title: Chiral fluorescent carbon dots for tyrosine enantiomers: Discrimination, mechanism and cell imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of chiral analytical methods for tyrosine enantiomers, characterized by superior recognition performance and selectivity, holds significant potential for application in the biochemical and biomedical domains associated with tyrosine. Herein, a straightforward and effective strategy for tyrosine enantiomers identification and purity analysis by chiral carbon dots (CDs) fluorescence sensor were reported. The chiral CDs were designed by using L-cysteine and 2,4-diaminophenol hydrochloride through a gentle one-step synthesis strategy at room temperature. The resulting chiral CDs exhibited completely different fluorescence response to L-tyrosine and D-tyrosine, that is, L-tyrosine could enhance the fluorescence of the chiral CDs, but D-tyrosine made no difference. The fluorescent differential recognition factor for enantiomers was as high as 23.2, which was the highest be achievement to our best knowledge so far. The varying discriminatory capabilities of chiral CDs towards tyrosine enantiomers were also applicable to HeLa cells. To confirm the recognition mechanism of chiral CDs to tyrosine enantiomers, the chiral CDs-modified SiO2 stationary phase were synthesized and applied to separate the tyrosine enantiomers in reverse-phase HPLC mode. Furthermore, density functional theory was combined with the fluorescence spectroscopy and chromatographic results to verify that the recognition ability was related to the difference of weak interaction energy and Gibbs free energy between the precursor molecule of the chiral CDs and the tyrosine enantiomers. This cost-effective and highly selectivity assay not only provided a valuable tool for tyrosine enantiomers identification and purity analysis, but also delivered an important reference for the recognition and purity identification of other chiral drugs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In this work, chiral fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared and applied for the discrimination and imaging of L-tyrosine and D-tyrosine. The mechanism of chiral discrimination was corroborated through the integration of high performance liquid chromatography, density functional theory, and fluorescence data.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The recognition of enantiomers through chirality is crucial, given that chirality is a prevalent and significant feature in numerous biomolecules and even within nature [1]. Chirality, a fundamental characteristic of the metabolic processes in living organisms, is also present in many active pharmaceutical molecules. Consequently, the identification of chiral molecules has garnered significant attention in fields such as biochemistry, pharmacy, and drug development. Amino acids are important biologically active molecules that compose enzymes and proteins in humans and animals, and the main biological components of active drug molecules. The chiral recognition of amino acids plays an extremely important role in pharmacology and chemical biology because different amino acid enantiomers could lead to different pharmacological activities and biological behaviors [2]. L-tyrosine, as the precursor of several neuro-transmitters such as dopamine, epinephrine and norepinephrine, could be used to alleviate albinism, alkaptonuria, hypochondrium, Parkinson’s disease and other mental diseases [3–5]. Inversely, D-tyrosine is used as a raw material for drug preparation and a probe for protein molecular conformation analysis [5]. Undoubtedly, it is crucial to develop methods to effectively identify amino acid enantiomers and monitor the purity of the enantiomers in order to accurately determine the physiological activity of each enantiomeric molecule and effectively monitor the enantiomeric purity or enantiomeric excess value (ee %) of chiral drugs in which research, development and manufacturing process.

The past few years have witnessed the application of different methods such as electrochemistry [4,6,7], HPLC [8], Rayleigh scattering spectroscopy [9], colorimetry [10], and fluorometry [11–15] in the identification of tyrosine enantiomers. Among these methods, electrochemistry and HPLC need complicated pretreatment and require expensive chiral columns. The weakness signal and directivity of Rayleigh scattering spectroscopy limited its wide practical application. Colorimetry was often limited by its low sensitivity. Inspiritingly, due to low cost, high sensitivity, simple and diverse signal output modes, fluorescence spectroscopy technology has attracted great attention from researchers [16–18]. At present, the constructed probes based on the fluorescence method to identify the tyrosine enantiomers included chiral tetraphenylethylene macrocycle amine [12], cholic acid-based fluorescent probes [19], BINOL-based near-IR fluorescent probe [13], AuNPs-β-CDs-FL [11], β-CD-QDs [14], L-NAC-CdSe/CdS QDs [15], β-CD/CdSe QDs [20], β-cyclodextrin-modified CdSe/ZnS QDs [21], etc. Significantly, the synthetic steps of the above-mentioned fluorescent probes were either complicated or the materials were more toxic. With the improvement of detection standards and the urgency of environmental protection, it was necessary to develop a simple, economical and efficient fluorescent probe that can effectively distinguish tyrosine enantiomers.

The key factor of chiral recognition was to construct a chiral nanomaterial probe. A universal and effective method to create chirality was to introduce a chiral source into nanomaterials. Carbon nanomaterials not only exhibited the excellent properties of organic small molecules and metal quantum dots, but also enjoyed low cost, abundant raw materials, low toxicity, easy functionalization, excellent water solubility, and has been widely used in sensing, imaging, antibacterial, antioxidant and other aspects [22–29]. Undoubtedly, chiral carbon nanomaterials will further demonstrate their chiral properties by inheriting those of traditional carbon nanomaterials. In this work, cheap and easily available L-cysteine and 2,4-diaminophenol hydrochloride were used as the chiral and carbon source to synthesize the chiral fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) at room temperature, respectively ( Scheme 1). The synthesized chiral fluorescent CDs were utilized for the identification of fluorescence spectra and the evaluation of enantiomeric purity in tyrosine enantiomers. Additionally, these analytical results enabled the detection of exogenous tyrosine enantiomers within HeLa cells using chiral CDs. To validate the chiral recognition mechanism, chiral CDs modified silica stationary phase was prepared and tyrosine enantiomers were separated by HPLC in reversed-phase mode. Subsequently, the density functional theory (DFT) was introduced to calculate the interaction between the building units of the chiral CDs and tyrosine enantiomers to explain the mechanism by which the chiral CDs recognize tyrosine enantiomers. Combined with fluorescence spectroscopy, chromatographic and DFT results, the recognition mechanism was analyzed in detail, that was, the difference of weak interaction energy and Gibbs free energy between the precursor molecule of the chiral CDs and the tyrosine enantiomers was mainly responsible for the recognition of the tyrosine enantiomers. This work provided a valuable tool for the identification and purity analysis of tyrosine enantiomers.

Chiral CDs were synthesized as follows. 2,4-Diaminophenol hydrochloride (3.0 mg) and L-cysteine (6.0 mg) were added orderly to 10 mL deionized water under stirring at room temperature. Subsequently, 1.0 mL of NaOH solution (1 M) was introduced dropwise into the above reaction system to promote the dissolution of the precursors. The reaction mixture was conducted at room temperature for 3 h, and the color changed gradually from colorless to faint yellow. After the reaction, the obtained products were purified by dialyzed with a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove unreacted precursors. The resultant chiral CDs were stored at 4 °C for further use. In addition, the purified chiral CDs were also freeze-dried for use in the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of chiral CDs-modified silica stationary phase
ce:section.ce:para: 3.0 g of commercial silica (diameter: 5 μm, pore size: 100 Å, surface area: 306 m2/g) was dispersed in HCl. Following a 24 h activation period, the silica was subjected to alternating washing with deionized water and ethanol until neutrality was achieved. Subsequently, it was dried in a vacuum oven at a temperature of 60 °C for an additional 24 h. Preferably, the obtained silica and 3-isocyanopropyl-triethoxysilane were sequentially dispersed in 60 mL of anhydrous acetonitrile, and the temperature was reduced to 55 °C after refluxing at 80 °C for 24 h. Immediately, 5.0 mL of the acetonitrile dispersion of the chiral CDs (20 mg/mL) and 0.3 mmol of triethylenediamine were added dropwise to the silica system to continue the reaction for 24 h. The resultant product was meticulously washed three times, alternately employing ethanol and deionized water, to eliminate any unreacted substances. Finally, the silica modified with the chiral CDs was vacuum dried at 55 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of the chiral CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, temperature was one of the key factors that destroy chirality in the fabrication process of chiral nanomaterials. However, cumbersome and time-consuming test conditions were usually optimized to maintain reliably the chiral characteristics of nanomaterials. Ingeniously, this work achieved the fabrication of the chiral CDs at room temperature (25 °C) by optimizing the mass ratio of 2,4-diaminophenol hydrochloride and L-cysteine and the reaction time between them (Fig. S1). Fig. S1A indicated that with the increase of the ratio of 2, 4-diaminophenol hydrochloride to L-cysteine, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased and then decreased after the ratio was greater than 1:2, which may be due to the cross-linking between excess L-cysteine and chiral CDs, resulting in the aggregation of chiral CDs and the weakening of fluorescence intensity. Fig. S1B manifested that the fluorescence intensity decreased when the reaction time was greater than 3 h, which may be mainly because the continuous agitation made the chiral CDs in high motion, resulting in an increase in the non-radiative transition process, and further led to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity. Compared with the circular dichroism (CD) of pure L-cysteine (Fig. S2A) and the previous report [30], the obtained chiral CDs showed a non-single CD signal peak (Fig. S2B). It can be seen that the signal of L-cysteine appeared at 200–245 nm, while the chiral CDs appeared at 200–294 nm, indicating that L-cysteine and 2,4-diaminophenol hydrochloride generated new CD signals at 200–294 nm after undergoing polymerization reaction. Apparently, the CD signal around 200–234 nm was inherited from the inherent chiral of the L-cysteine, and the new CD signal at 235–294 nm was generated by the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain placed in a chiral environment [31,32]. The resultant chiral CDs exhibited a fluorescence emission peak at 490 nm under excitation of 395 nm in Fig. S3 and a excitation independent emission behavior of the chiral CDs was also observed (Fig. S4), indicating the size of chiral CDs was relatively uniform [33]. As revealed in Fig. S3, the absorption band at ∼266 nm in the UV-Vis spectrum was ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [34]. A absorption peak at ∼330 nm was caused by surface function groups that passivate the surface traps [35]. In addition, there was a broad absorption band ∼420 nm, relating to the pernigraniline-like structure of 2,4-diaminophenol hydrochloride [34]. Transmission electron micro-scopy (TEM) was used to provide information about morphology, particle size distribution of the chiral CDs. It can be seen from Fig. 1A that the chiral CDs exhibited excellent non-aggregation properties. The particle size of the chiral CDs was estimated by counting randomly on the TEM images. The diameter distribution was mainly 2.6–3.8 nm, and the average particle size was 3.2 nm (Fig. 1B). Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 1C illustrated that the chiral CDs have a crystalline structure, which was attributed to the interaction between the atoms within these chiral CDs and the high-speed electron beam, analogous to the diffraction of light waves within a crystal. XRD analysis results based on phase recognition showed that the chiral CDs have a crystalline structure, which was consistent with the SAED results (Fig. 1D). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is a surface chemical analysis technique that provided information about the surface characteristics, element composition and electronic state of the elements present in the material by irradiating the sample with X-ray beam. To characterize the chiral CDs, XPS and Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were combined to evaluate the elemental composition and chemical bonds of the chiral CDs. In detail, the XPS analysis data was as follows. Binding energy calibration was based on C 1 s at 284.6 eV. XPS survey spectrum revealed that the chiral CDs were mainly composed of C, N, O and S elements (Fig. 1E). The elemental content analysis results for the chiral CDs acquired by XPS were in Table S1. The high resolution S 2p XPS spectrum confirmed two main bands at 162.8 and 168.5 eV, the former was belonged to the S 2p3/2 of the C-S-C covalent bond, and the latter was assigned to the S 2p1/2 of the C-SO3 species (Fig. 1F) [36,37]. The high resolution C 1 s spectrum shown in Fig. 1G could be resolved as follows: C-C/CC at 284.6 eV; C-N, C-O and C-S at 285.0 eV; CO at 289.1 eV [36,38–40]. The high resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1H) had two peaks at 398.7 and 400.1 eV, which assigned to pyridinc N and pyrrolic N, respectively [41,42]. As shown in Fig. 1I, the high resolution O 1 s spectrum displayed two peaks centered at 531.5 and 536.1 eV, which associated with CO and OC-OH, respectively [36]. FTIR in Fig. S5 further verified the presence of the O-H, N-H, C-H, S-H, -CON-, C-N and C-S. Both XPS and FTIR manifested that the chiral CDs were successfully prepared. The above results verified that the chiral CDs contained -SH, -OH, -NH2 and sp2 unsaturated structure.

The stability of fluorescent nanomaterials determined its performance in subsequent studies, and excellent stability could provide a guarantee for the accuracy of the test. It can be seen from Fig. S6 that the NaCl solution with a concentration of up to 1000 mM hardly affected the fluorescence intensity of the chiral CDs, which enabled the detection of chiral tyrosine with the chiral CDs probe in a high ionic strength system. In addition, the influence of pH on the chiral CDs has also been investigated. Fig. S7 shown that when the pH increased from 5.5 to 9.5, the fluorescence emission wavelength of the chiral CDs gradually red-shifted, attributable to the alterations in the conjugated and rigid structures of chiral CDs induced by the interaction between sodium hydrate ions and chiral CDs. XPS and FTIR displayed that the chiral CDs contained phenolic hydroxyl and amino groups. When the pH was low, the functional groups on the surface of the chiral CDs were mainly protonated. However, when the pH gradually increased, deprotonation caused the H+ in the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of the chiral CDs to leave, causing the chiral CDs to be negatively charged. On the other hand, H2O could combine with Na+ in PBS to form positively charged hydrated sodium ions, which could further combine with negatively charged chiral CDs to obtain hydrated sodium ionized chiral CDs with larger particle size [43]. As a result, the fluorescence emission wavelength of the chiral CDs exhibited a pH-dependent red-shift effect, which was consistent with the phenomenon that the emission wavelength of fluorescent nanomaterials would red-shifted as its particle size increased [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of chiral CDs-modified silica stationary phase
ce:section.ce:para: To identify L and D-tyrosine by HPLC, the resultant chiral CDs were modified to the spherical porous silica (diameter: 5 μm, pore size: 100 Å, surface area: 306 m2 /g) surface under optimal conditions ( Fig. 2A). In cases where the measurements exceeded the resolution of scanning electron microscopies (1–20 nm), it was probable to use the TEM to provide a much higher resolution (about 0.2 nm) [46,47]. TEM image in Fig. S8 showed that the chiral CDs were successfully modified onto the surface of the silica. EDX results in Fig. S9 showed that in addition to the Si and O elements contained in the silica, the C, N and S elements were also presented, further verifying that the silica modified with the chiral CDs was successfully prepared. Subsequently, the obtained chiral CDs-modified silica was packed into a stainless-steel column, which was used for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescence method to identifytyrosine enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the sensitivity of the chiral CDs probe to recognize chiral tyrosine, L and D-tyrosine were introduced into the chiral CDs solution, respectively. Fig. 2B shown that L-tyrosine could significantly enhance the fluorescence of the probe system, while D-tyrosine did not induce this effect. In particular, the fluorescence emission wavelengths exhibited blue shift after adding L-tyrosine or D-tyrosine to the chiral CDs. This phenomenon could be explained as: The -OH, -NH2, and sp2 unsaturated carbon in chiral CDs may have intermolecular forces with -OH, -NH2 and -CC- in tyrosine, resulting in more electron donor groups and n-π* electron transition in the resultant product. Since the interaction process between chiral CDs and tyrosine occurred in a polar solvent (PBS), this interaction will reduce the energy level (E0) of the electrons in the ground state while the energy level of the excited electron remained unchanged (E1), which caused the increase of transition energy level △E (E1-E0) and the blue shifted of UV-Vis wavelength, which further induced the blue shift of fluorescence wavelength. Furthermore, when 10 mM L-tyrosine was added to the probe solution, the fluorescence intensity reached a maximum within 30 s and hardly changed as time prolonged (Fig. S10). This also showed that the response time of the chiral CDs probe to L-tyrosine was very fast and suitable for on-site testing requirements. Conversely, 14 mM D-tyrosine caused a negligible increase in the fluorescence intensity of the chiral CDs probe within 30 s, and would not affect the fluorescence intensity of the probe solution due to the prolonged time (Fig. S11). Importantly, the fluorescence intensity of the chiral CDs enhanced gradually with the increasing concentration of L-tyrosine from 0.5 to 14 mM (Fig. 2C). Fig. 2D shown an excellent linear relationship between the F/F
0 value (F and F
0 represented the fluorescence intensity of the presence and absence of analytes, respectively.) and the concentration of L-tyrosine (R2=0.998), with the limit of the detection (LOD) (3 s/k, in which s is the standard deviation for the blank solution and k is the slope of the calibration curve) of 0.16 mM, the recognition difference value (F L-F 0)/(F D-F 0) reached a maximum (23.2) compared with the reported works ( Table 1) [4–15,48–51]. However, when the concentration of D-tyrosine was increased from 0.5 to 14 mM, the fluorescence intensity of the chiral CDs probe system in which it was existed had a negligible change (Fig. S12). The above results indicated that the fluorescence method based on the chiral CDs could distinguish effectively L and D-tyrosine. In addition, the role of L-cysteine in recognizing chiral tyrosine has also been verified. The recognition performance of chiral tyrosine from the reaction product of L-cysteine or 2,4-diaminophenol hydrochloride was investigated separately. Fig. S13 shown that the reaction product of L-cysteine has no fluorescence and its fluorescence performance has not changed after the introduction of L or D-tyrosines. Similarly, chiral tyrosine would not cause changed in the fluorescence properties of the reaction product of 2,4-diaminophenol hydrochloride (Fig. S14), further indicating that L-cysteine was a necessary condition for the formation of the chiral CDs and its effective recognition of chiral tyrosine. Subsequently, the selectivity of the constructed chiral CDs probe for recognizing chiral tyrosine was conducted. Fig. 2E shown that in addition to L-tyrosine, D-tyrosine and other potential chiral interferences (L/D-tartaric acid, L/D-aminopropanol, L/D-phenylalanine, R/S-mandelic acid, L/D-alanine, L/D-asparagine, L/D-glutamic acid, L/D-histidine, L/D-tryptophan) would not affect the fluorescence intensity of the chiral CDs, further verifying the excellent performance of the probe. In addition, the CIE coordinate (Fig. S15) further manifested the tremendous potential of the chiral CDs as fluorescent labeling platforms in biological systems.

To evidence the recognition mechanism, the chiral CDs modified silica stationary phases were synthesized, characterized and applied for the separation of tyrosine enantiomers in HPLC. To achieve the best separation performance of chiral tyrosine from HPLC, the polarity and flow rate of the mobile phase have been optimized. For the mobile phase methanol/water (70/30, v/v), the retention time of the tyrosine enantiomers decreased when the flow rate increased from 0.2 to 0.6 mL/min (Fig. S16). In Fig. S17 and S18, when the mobile phase methanol/water (90/10, v/v) and methanol/water (80/20, v/v) were used separately, the retention time of the tyrosine enantiomers was similar to the separation performance in Fig. S16. Interestingly, for the mobile phase methanol/water (80/20, v/v), an optimal separation performance of tyrosine enantiomers was achieved when the flow rate was 0.4 mL/min ( Fig. 3A and B). This may be caused by the change of methanol content in the mobile phase that affected the chiral recognition ability of the modified chiral CDs on the stationary phase to tyrosine enantiomers.

From the separation results (especially the retention time) in the chromatograms (Fig. 3A and B) that the peak sequence of D-tyrosine was earlier than that of L-tyrosine, indicating that L-tyrosine has a relatively strong interaction with the chiral CDs on the stationary phase. In addition, the fluorescence spectrum showed that the addition of L-tyrosine would increase the fluorescence of the chiral CDs (Fig. 2C). This further showed that a relatively strong force between L-tyrosine and the chiral CDs was exhibited, which lead to the surface of the chiral CDs covered more L-tyrosine and caused the free rotation of covered CDs to be blocked and further improved the fluorescence efficiency of the chiral CDs. Furthermore, to verify the chiral recognition mechanism, the FTIR data were provided of the structure of the complexes formed between the chiral CDs and tyrosine enantiomers. Fig. S19 illustrated that the incorporation of L- or D-tyrosine into chiral CDs resulted in a shift of the C-N stretching vibration towarded lower wave numbers (from 1297 cm−1 to 1271 cm−1 or 1297 cm−1 to 1268 cm−1), while simultaneously enhancing the intensity of the C-S stretching vibration at 1245 cm−1. The C-S stretching vibration intensity was more pronounced in chiral CDs + L-tyrosine than in chiral CDs + D-tyrosine. These findings indicated an increase in C-S content following the addition of L-tyrosine to chiral CDs, may be represent a stronger binding affinity of chiral CDs for L-tyrosine. The observed interactions between -SH in chiral CDs and -COOH of tyrosine may suggest the formation of the structure depicted in Fig. S20 between chiral CDs and tyrosine. Subsequently, density functional theory (DFT) analysis method was applied to analyze the interaction between the main building units of the chiral CDs and the enantiomers of tyrosine. The reason for choosing the main building unit of the chiral CDs as model molecules was that there was no accurate statement about the structure of CDs so far. Therefore, choosing chiral CDs as the calculation model may lead to some incorrect conclusions. To obtain the lowest energy conformation of L-cysteine, tyrosine enantiomers, L-cysteine/L-tyrosine, and L-cysteine/D-tyrosine, a conformational search was carried out by using Lu Tian's molclus program, the corresponding results were illustrated in Fig. 3C-G [52]. Hereon, all DFT calculations were performed with the Gaussian 09 programs [53]. The B3LYP-D3(BJ) functional method was employed with the def2-TZVP basis set for geometry optimizations. Moreover, vibrational frequencies were computed at the same level to verify no imaginary frequency in energy minima. Meanwhile, considering BSSE effect, the weak interaction energy between molecules was calculated. Relevant data were processed by Lu Tian's Multiwfn software package and the corresponding data were shown in Table S2 [54]. The results demonstrated that the weak interaction energy and Gibbs free energy between L-cysteine and L-tyrosine were significantly smaller than those between L-cysteine and D-tyrosine, indicating that the interaction force between L-cysteine and L-tyrosine was stronger. In addition, the IRI method to graphically examine chemical bonds and weak interactions in the chemical system was also used to visualize the interaction between L-cysteine and L/D-tyrosine, and the IRI data were consistent with the above calculation results (Fig. 3H-J) [55]. These results further verified the mechanism of identifying chiral tyrosine by fluorescence method and HPLC method based on the chiral CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition of L-tyrosine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the application performance of the established method, the standard addition method was applied to detect L-tyrosine in three bagged milk samples. The recoveries of the three samples changed from 99.0 % to 103.0 %, and the RSD fluctuated in the range of 2.2–6.3 % ( Table 2). The above results verify that the method exhibited good practical application potential in complex real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of chiral CDs in the racemic solution
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the developed chiral fluorescent CDs probe was evaluated by quantitative analysis of L-tyrosine in the racemate solution. Interestingly, an excellent proportional relationship between the ee value of L-tyrosine and the fluorescence intensity was presented (Fig. S21A), and a good linearity appeared between the fluorescence intensity change value and the ee value in range of 0–80 % (Fig. S21B). Therefore, the chiral CDs could be a reliable tool used for the quantitative analysis purity of L-tyrosine in racemic solutions.

To investigate the application potential of the chiral CDs in biological systems, the cytotoxicity of the chiral CDs to HeLa cells was evaluated by MTT assay, the details were similar to what we have reported [56]. Fig. S22 shown that when the concentration of the chiral CDs was increased from 0 to 400 μg/mL, the cell viability still exceeded 80 %. This phenomenon manifested that the chiral CDs show excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity to HeLa cells, which was beneficial for living cells imaging and also proved the application potential of the chiral CDs in biological systems. The subsequent investigation focused on the uptake rate of chiral CDs by HeLa cells. Fig. S23 and Fig. S24 demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity progressively increased in correlation with the extended co-incubation time between the chiral CDs and HeLa cells, peaking at 2.0 h. However, as the co-incubation time continued to extend, the fluorescence intensity began to decrease gradually. Consequently, it was determined that the optimal cellular uptake rate occurred when the cells were co-incubated with chiral CDs for a duration of 2.0 h. Successively, the impact of L- and D-tyrosine on the fluorescence intensity of chiral CDs was examined in HeLa cells. As depicted in Fig. 4A-C, chiral CDs emitted yellow fluorescence in HeLa cells when the emission wavelength of the channel was 559 nm. Upon co-incubation of HeLa cells with 14 mM D-tyrosine and chiral CDs for 2 h, the fluorescence signal exhibited consistency with that observed in the presence of chiral CDs alone (Fig. 4D-F). However, upon co-incubation of HeLa cells with 14 mM L-tyrosine and chiral CDs, there was a significant enhancement in the fluorescence signal (Fig. 4G-I). Consequently, these findings designated that chiral CDs can effectively recognize exogenous tyrosine enantiomers within HeLa cells, thereby indicating their potential as bioimaging reagents in the fields of biochemistry and biomedicine.

In conclusion, a fluorescent and HPLC dual-readout assay was developed to sensitively and selectively recognize the tyrosine enantiomers and determine its enantiomeric excess value based on a satisfactory chiral CDs prepared by a facile and gentle one-step process and the chiral CDs modified HPLC stationary phase. The established dual-readout pattern not only made the analytical results more convincing but also broadened the application of the spectral sensors. DFT calculation has also been used to combine HPLC separation results and fluorescence data to verify the mechanism of chiral CDs efficiently recognizing tyrosine enantiomers. Of note, the smaller Gibbs free energy and weak interaction energy between the precursor molecule L-cysteine of the chiral CDs and L-tyrosine was mainly responsible for the distinction between L- and D-tyrosine by the chiral CDs. Furthermore, the synthesized chiral fluorescent CDs displayed excellent biocompatibility and minimal toxicity towards HeLa cells, this allowed for effective recognition of exogenous tyrosine enantiomers within these cells. Additionally, the CDs demonstrated robust applicability in complex real milk samples. These findings underscored the potential utility of chiral fluorescent CDs in biological systems, including biochemistry and biomedicine.

Hongdeng Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yan-ping Shi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Manchang Kou: Resources, Formal analysis. Haixia Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yangxia Han: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174153 and 22374160) and CAS-JSPS Cooperation and Exchange Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences (No. 121B62KYSB20210012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136677.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and electrochemical chiral sensing of the robust sandwich chiral composite d-His-ZIF-8@Au@ZIF-8

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enantioselective glutamic acid discrimination and nanobiological imaging by chiral fluorescent silicon nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Han, M. Kou, K., Quan, J. Wang, H. Zhang, H. Ihara, et al., Enantioselective glutamic acid discrimination and nanobiological imaging by chiral fluorescent silicon nanoparticles, Anal. Chem. 96 (2024) 2173-2182.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yangxia Han received her master's degree from Lanzhou University in 2018 (PR China). Her current research interests are the design and synthesis of novel fluorescent nanomaterials and their application in separation and analysis.

Manchang Kou got his bachelor’s degree from China University of Mining and Technology, China. Now, he is pursuing master’s degree in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, China. His current research is focused on phosphorescent chemosensors.

Haixia Zhang is a professor of the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Lanzhou University. Her research interests are the application of new materials in sample pretreatment, the application of new material in drug carrier and investigation of fluorescent sensors.

Hongdeng Qiu is a full professor in Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He received his Ph. D. from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics and had a postdoctoral position in Kumamoto University from 2009 to 2012 (JSPS Fellow). His current research interests are the applications of new materials in chromatography and biosensors, especially ionic liquids and nanomaterials for separation and detection of complex analytes. He has published more than 160 papers and 30 patents.

Yan-Ping Shi received his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University, PR China, in 1996. He is currently a professor Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese academy of Sciences (CAS), PR China. His research interests focus on discovering new high active drug from natural medicine plant, developing complex sample pretreatment and fluorescent chemo sensors.
